subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, discipline-relevant title with consistently reiterated focus on government transport initiatives in Hyderabad
Explicit, time-bounded, well-connected research question that guides hypotheses and conclusions
Well-justified mixed-methods approach showing evidence of informed source selection
weaknesses: Minor scope drift between âgrowthâ and âdevelopmentâ in early pages
Insufficient discussion of model limitations and uncertainty
Inconsistent unit labeling in COâ calculations and unexplained data extrapolations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the topic accurately, states a focused research question, and employs an appropriate range of sources and methods. Minor breadth in early framing and lack of critical discussion around model assumptions prevent a top-band 6 but justify a strong band-5 score.

strengths: Effective application of quantitative data (registrations, ridership, PM2.5) with clear citations and interpretation
Solid integration of economic concepts (externalities, TOD) in context
Generally accurate use of economic terminology and IB mathematical tools
weaknesses: Occasional mis-labelling in diagrams (MPB vs MSC) and minor notation/spelling errors
Bibliography contains duplicates and a few unsourced estimates
Could improve source reliability by citing more primary government data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge, with mostly appropriate and relevant source use. Minor inaccuracies in labeling and consistency issues limit seamless execution, placing it at the lower end of band-5 but above band-4.

strengths: Consistently appropriate and relevant research tied to the question
Effective data analysis with well-supported conclusions from regressions and cost-benefit tables
Logical, well-structured argument with partially critical evaluation of survey bias and data gaps
weaknesses: Survey sample size is small and unrepresentative, lacking confidence intervals or demographic analysis
No significance tests (p-values, CIs) provided for regression outputs
Limited consideration of non-transport pollution sources and alternative explanations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student moves beyond description to clear, mostly well-supported analysis and evaluation, constructing a reasoned argument. While critical evaluation is present, gaps in statistical rigor and alternative factor analysis prevent full top-band performance, supporting a high band-7-9 score (9/12).

strengths: Consistent formatting (word count, page numbers, contents) and MLA citations throughout
Appropriate structure following EE conventions, with clear sectioning and integrated visuals
weaknesses: Some figure captions conflate tables and charts
A few charts lack labeled axes or units
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets all EE requirements with clear structure, consistent layout, and correct formatting. Minor issues in figure labeling do not detract from overall clarity, warranting the maximum band-3-4 score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly presented, time-bounded and uses economic language (âTo what extent hasâ¦contributedâ¦2019-2024â).  Throughout the essay the student keeps returning to this question when structuring hypotheses, analysis and conclusion, indicating a strong connection to the ensuing discussion.  Although causal language is ambitious for an EE and some variables are not fully isolated, the clarity and focus meet the upper-band descriptor.

The student states a concise, discipline-relevant title and explains the contextual importance of sustainable transport in Hyderabad.  Purpose and focus (impact of specific government transport initiatives 2019-24) are consistently reiterated across introduction, methodology and conclusion, showing a clear, appropriate framing.  Minor breadth (growth versus development) lingers in early pages, but does not blur the central focus, so the descriptor for the 5-6 band is met, albeit not with absolute sophistication.

Most quantitative data inserted in charts are directly applied to answer the RQ (vehicle registrations, ridership, PM2.5).  Citations are given, graphs interpreted, and survey results incorporated.  Nevertheless, a few figures are estimated without clear sourcing and the bibliography contains duplicates, limiting consistency.  Application is therefore âmostly relevant and appropriateâ, fitting the 3-4 band.

The report follows EE conventionsâtitle page, abstract omitted but intro/method/discussion/conclusion, contents list, consistent section numbering, appendices.  Figures and tables are integrated and referenced.  Minor issues (duplicate references, some long paragraphs) do not undermine overall clarity; thus structure is âclearly appropriateâ, warranting top band score 4.

Word count, table of contents, page numbers and sub-headings are present; fonts and spacing are consistent; citations follow MLA-style.  Visuals are labelled.  Therefore layout and formatting meet all stipulated EE requirements, aligning with the 3-4 band; a score of 4 reflects the neat execution.

Arguments are logically sequencedâhypothesis, empirical test, refutation of environmental claim, policy recommendations.  The student does acknowledge survey bias and data gaps, showing partial critical evaluation, but the assessment of alternative explanations (COVID, income growth) is brief.  This fits âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ at the upper end of 4-6.

The essay moves from data description to regression output, explains correlations, contrasts with PM2.5 trend, and incorporates cost-benefit tables.  Conclusions usually follow from evidence, yet causation versus correlation is not fully resolved and statistical limitations are thinly evaluated.  Depth is therefore âclear â¦ mostly supportedâ, meriting a 5 within the 4-6 band.

The student demonstrates solid understanding of negative externalities, welfare loss, positive externalities and TOD concepts, and links them to empirical context.  Occasional mis-labelling (âsocial cost on price axisâ) and over-statement of causality reveal partial weaknesses, so knowledge is âclearâ but not consistently rigorous â appropriate for band 3-4.

The majority of secondary statistics are relevant to Hyderabad or Telangana during 2019-24 and clearly tied to the hypothesis.  While some newspaper sources are of moderate reliability, the overall body of evidence is appropriate and clearly linked, satisfying the 4-6 descriptor; mid-band score reflects remaining validity gaps.

A mixed-methods approach (secondary data sets, simple regressions, primary survey) is described and justified.  Source selection shows some thought (government portals, L&T reports) but reliability concerns remain â e.g., extrapolated 2025 figures, small non-representative survey, newspaper compilations.  Statistical tools are basic and the student does not discuss limitations of linear models.  Hence the work aligns with âgenerally relevantâ¦some evidence of informed selectionâ, upper end of 3-4 band rather than fully âeffectiveâ.

Economic terminology (MPB, MSC, Pearsonâs r, TCO) is used frequently and generally accurately.  Minor spelling/notation errors (margnial, MPB2 on demand curve) prevent full consistency, so performance aligns with âmostly accurateâ rather than the seamless precision expected at the 5-6 level.

annotations.comment: The studentâs initial reflection effectively outlines how the research question was refined, but remains largely descriptive. To deepen evaluative insight, the student should critique how initial methodological choices shaped outcomes and consider alternative approaches.

The methodology description highlights use of regression and Pearsonâs coefficient, showing strong mathematical integration. This clarity in analytic tools demonstrates effective application of IB math concepts.
The table of contents follows conventions and clearly maps the structure, aiding navigation. This demonstrates strong presentation and structure.
Only 61 survey responses are collected, limiting representativeness. The student should note confidence intervals and demographic biases to critically appraise primary data quality.
The concept of market failure is explained with clarity, but the demand curve labeling misplaces MPB2; ensure marginal private and social benefit labels match standard economic diagrams.
Figure 4âs caption conflates table and chart elements; a distinct title and clear description of axes would improve interpretability and presentation coherence.

The student acknowledges survey limitations in the reflection, but could strengthen critical evaluation by quantifying bias and suggesting how sampling could be broadened. Explicit consideration of representativeness is needed.

The final reflection lists challenges and skills gained but remains largely descriptive. To meet higher-band reflection, include evaluative discussion of how setbacks informed conceptual understanding or altered the research plan.
The title page correctly states the research question, discipline, and word count, fully meeting IB formatting requirements.
Footnote markup appears as raw HTML entity tags (e.g., âlt;sup>â), suggesting OCR or formatting errors. Ensure superscripts render correctly in the final version.
The discussion of megacities and sustainability frameworks shows clear understanding of economic concepts and relevance to the research context.
Reference to Hyderabadâs population and metro details is well integrated, but citing a local census or government report would strengthen source reliability beyond Statista.

The hypothesis section includes quantitative projections (e.g., annual COâ savings) but does not discuss uncertainty or model limitations. A brief sensitivity analysis would strengthen methodological rigor.
Calculations of COâ savings use clear formulas, but units (kg vs tonnes) should be consistently labeled and discussed to avoid confusion in the costâbenefit analysis.
The methodology clearly outlines data sources and regression models, showing informed selection. However, discuss why linear models were chosen over alternatives to justify model appropriateness.
Table 3 includes predicted average values for FebâMar 2025 without describing the extrapolation method. Provide regression model details or source of prediction to justify these estimates.
Figure 14âs costâbenefit table mixes g/km and g per vehicle without clarifying passenger assumptions. Add a brief note on occupancy rates used in calculations.
Survey data are presented visually, but the axes and scales are not labeled on some charts (e.g., Figures 17â20). Ensure all graphs include axis titles and units.
The regression outputs show strong negative correlations (Rââ0.9), but no p-values or confidence intervals are provided. Include significance tests to support causal claims.
The discussion acknowledges that PM2.5 worsens despite transport initiatives but omits consideration of non-transport pollution sources. A cross-sector analysis would strengthen critical evaluation.
The cost-of-ownership analysis correctly shows EV savings, demonstrating effective economic evaluation. The comparison is coherent and supports conclusions well.
Conclusion restates findings but could more critically evaluate limitations and propose how future research might isolate variables (e.g., control for income growth vs COVID impacts).
Bibliography contains duplicate and incomplete entries (e.g., repeated TSRTC citation). Deduplicate and ensure consistent MLA formatting throughout.
Appendix contains administrative survey forms which do not contribute to analysis. Consider summarizing key questionnaire items instead of full form to improve readability.
